one_line_takeaway: A two-architecture multi-agent (Coordinator pre-input gating + Guard post-output validation) defense that reports reducing prompt-injection ASR from 20-30% to 0% on a self-built 55-attack / 400-run benchmark, though the perfect-mitigation result is undercut by a small self-authored, self-graded dataset and train-on-test circularity in the rule-based variant.
key_finding: Across 400 evaluations, every defense configuration reduced Attack Success Rate (ASR) to 0%, a claimed 100% mitigation. Undefended baselines were exploitable at 20-30% ASR (abstract: 30% ChatGLM, 20% Llama2). Baseline vulnerability was highly uneven by category: Delegate attacks 100% ASR, Role-play 66.7%, Recon/Environment 60%, Directory 50%, Data Exfiltration 50%, Obfuscation 33.3%, Formatting 20%, while Override, Context Leak, and CTA/Navigation were 0% even without defenses. The authors conclude defense success is driven by comprehensive detection rather than architectural sophistication (all three variants converged to identical 0% ASR).
core_contribution: The paper proposes a multi-agent LLM defense pipeline against prompt injection, instantiated in two complementary architectures: a chain-of-agents pipeline (a Domain LLM generates a candidate answer that a downstream Guard agent mandatorily vets for policy/format violations before release) and a coordinator pipeline (a Coordinator agent classifies and gates user input up front, issuing a safe refusal for flagged inputs and routing benign ones to the Domain LLM). The authors also build the HPI_ATTACK_DATASET (55 unique prompt injection attacks across 8 categories, expanded to 400 attack instances) and a full deployment reference architecture (API Gateway, Event Orchestrator, Coordinator, Guard, two buffer stages, Policy Store, logging). This is the paper's OWN contribution; the four-category defense taxonomy (input sanitization, output monitoring, prompt engineering, model-level defenses) and prior multi-agent security work (Muliarevych [26], Gosmar et al. [27]) are cited Related Work, not their result.
threat_model: Adversary embeds malicious instructions in user input to an LLM-integrated application to override the system prompt and induce unintended behavior. Attack goals span 8 categories: direct overrides, code execution, data exfiltration, formatting attacks, obfuscation (e.g., base64-encoded payloads), tool/agent manipulation (delegation), role-play coercion, and multi-turn persistence. An attack counts as successful if the model produces any output consistent with the attack's intended failure mode (override, leakage, coercion, recon, exfil, etc.). Evaluation is almost entirely single-turn (except a small persistence subset); indirect injection via external content is acknowledged as out of scope / an open challenge.
limitations: The headline result (0% ASR / 100% mitigation across all 400 runs, zero variance) is implausibly perfect and rests on a small hand-built dataset (only 55 unique attacks) that the authors both authored and human-graded, creating clear evaluation circularity, most starkly for the Taxonomy Filter, which is a rule-based matcher built from patterns of the very HPI dataset it is then tested on (train-on-test leakage). The paper reports no utility/false-positive rate on legitimate queries beyond an assertion that functionality is preserved, and gives no latency, cost, or throughput numbers despite claiming scalability trade-offs (Fig. 8 is qualitative). Per-category case counts in Table V (e.g., Override 60, CTA 60, Recon 50) are internally inconsistent with the stated dataset composition (12 direct overrides, 7 exfiltration, etc.), so the exact denominators are unclear. Evaluation is single-turn; indirect, adaptive, and multi-turn attacks are explicitly left as open challenges. Only two older models (2022/2023) are tested.

datasets_used: HPI_ATTACK_DATASET (authors' own): 55 unique prompt injection attacks across 8 categories, expanded to 400 attack instances
Attack category composition: Direct Overrides (12), Code Execution (8), Data Exfiltration (7), Formatting (6), Obfuscation (8), Tool/Agent Manipulation (5), Role-Play (6), Multi-Turn Persistence (3)
Evaluation suites: v1 Taxonomy (25 cases), Phase 2 Chain (15 cases), Phase 2 Coordinator (15 cases)
quantitative_results: Baseline (undefended) ASR: 30% for ChatGLM, 20% for Llama2 (abstract)
Table IV: v1 Taxonomy Filter Guard OFF = 30/100 = 30.0% ASR, Guard ON = 0/100 = 0.0% (100% reduction)
Table IV: Phase2 Coordinator Guard OFF = 10/50 = 20.0% ASR, Guard ON = 0/50 = 0.0% (100% reduction)
Table IV: Phase2 Chain Guard OFF = 15/50 = 30.0% ASR, Guard ON = 0/50 = 0.0% (100% reduction)

Category baseline ASR (Table V): Delegate 100.0%, Role-play 66.7%, Recon/Environment 60.0%, Directory 50.0%, Data Exfiltration 50.0%, Obfuscation 33.3%, Formatting 20.0%, Override 0.0%, Context Leak 0.0%, CTA/Navigation 0.0% — all reduced to 0.0% when protected
Total: 400 evaluations, 55 unique attack types, all defenses achieved 0% ASR
baselines_compared: Undefended system (direct query forwarding with default prompts)
Taxonomy-based rule filter (baseline defense) vs. Chain-of-Agents pipeline vs. Coordinator pipeline
Two model platforms: ChatGLM-6B (2022) and Llama2-13B (2023)
